summary: Stancil argues that data teams inside startups operate like collectivist service orgs, which leads to fuzzy prioritization and hidden politics. He proposes an internal market with credits and auctions to price data work, claiming it could allocate effort more efficiently and transparently while still letting teams steer strategy. He ends cautiously, noting the model could work with safeguards but needs regulation to avoid a cutthroat culture.
money_quotes: Nobody is a better judge of a data asset’s worth than its consumer.
An open auction doesn’t just create a market for the work itself, however; it also prices in a lot of other intangible tradeoffs that data teams often have to make.
A transparent bidding system in which everyone can see what bids the team accepts and at what cost they accept them may actually create a healthier dynamic than one built on argument and influence.
As promising as a free market for analysis might be, on today of all days, I’m still voting for a bit more regulation.
key_insight: A credit-and-auction market for data work could replace political prioritization with transparent price signals, but it needs guardrails to avoid perverse incentives.
